Client 2: And I just want to make sure we’re not, like, accidentally living too close to the edge while he’s between jobs.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to what you just said, Rober—runway—let’s start with the basics: about what’s your household gross income in a normal year?
Client 1: Normal year, all-in, it’s about two-eighteen a year. Roughly $218,600.
Client 2: Wait, is that gross or what actually hits the checking account?
Advisor: Good catch—gross, before taxes and deductions. Net would be lower. For planning, I’m asking gross first. Does $218,600 gross sound right for the household?

Advisor: Perfect, that nuance helps. Let’s pivot to spending. About what do you two spend per month total—mortgage or rent, groceries, everything—just the household burn rate.
Client 2: We’re around, I don’t know, maybe $7,000 a month?
Client 1: It’s a bit higher—$7,200 a month is what we’ve been using.
Advisor: Okay, $7,200 per month total expenses. Elizabeth, when you said $7,000—was that excluding anything, like travel or insurance?
Client 2: I think I was excluding the bigger annual stuff. But yeah, if you average it out, $7,200 is fair.

Client 2: And I just want to make sure we’re not, like, accidentally living too close to the edge while he’s between jobs.

Client 1: Yep, $0 outstanding.

Client 1: Total investable is about $611,200.
Advisor: Okay, $611,200. Let’s break that into buckets so we’re not hand-waving. Do you have a taxable brokerage account—joint or individual?
Client 1: Yes, joint taxable brokerage—about $293,950.

Client 1: Correct. Monthly debt cost is $0.

Client 1: Correct, PA.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, I’m going to pivot to taxes for a second—just the bracket you tend to land in. Do you know roughly what federal tax bracket you’re in these days?
Client 2: Uh, I always think it’s like… thirty-something? Is that the one where you take home 37%?
Advisor: Close, but not quite—37% is the bracket rate, not what you take home. The question is: do you typically fall into the 37% federal bracket, or a different one?
Client 1: It’s 37%—top bracket for us, at least in the years with the bonus.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. I got laid off recently, so I’m trying to sanity-check the plan—retirement timing, cash runway, all that.
Client 2: And I just want to make sure we’re not, like, accidentally living too close to the edge while he’s between jobs.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to what you just said, Rober—runway—let’s start with the basics: about what’s your household gross income in a normal year?
Client 1: Normal year, all-in, it’s about two-eighteen a year. Roughly $218,600.

Client 1: Yep, I’m Client 1.
Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yes.
Advisor: Great. Now, how does that income break down between you two—ballpark is fine.
Client 2: I’m the steady one. I make, like, around fifty-ish.
Client 1: It’s $48,650 gross for Elizabeth, if I’m remembering right.

Client 2: And I just want to make sure we’re not, like, accidentally living too close to the edge while he’s between jobs.

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Going back to what you said earlier about wanting a clear next step, Rober, I want to zoom out for two quick household basics that affect how I frame the plan.
Client 1: Sure, go ahead.
Advisor: First one’s simple: you two are married, right? Like legally married or civil partners—same household.
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Client 1: Married, yes.

Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Client 1: Married, yes.
Advisor: Great. Second—risk comfort. Earlier you both sounded more growth-leaning, but I want to hear it in your words: would you say your overall risk tolerance is “growth,” “balanced,” or “conservative”?
Client 1: Growth. I mean, not reckless, but growth.
Client 2: I’m more cautious than he is, but—yeah, overall we’ve been growth.

Client 1: Yes, US.
Client 2: Yep, US.
Advisor: And the market you’re looking for help in—also US-based RIA style, right? Like a US registered investment advisor relationship, not a bank product pitch.
Client 2: Wait—RIA is like… an IRA? Sorry, I always mix those up.
Client 1: No—RIA is the advisor type. IRA is the retirement account.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?

Client 1: Mine was about $169,950 gross.
Advisor: Okay. And I’m hearing there’s also some kind of bonus component—how does that show up for you, Rober?
Client 1: Yeah, it was structured as a monthly bonus line. It’s weird, but it annualizes to that $218,600 number.

Client 1: Correct—Client 1.
Client 2: Yes, I’m Client 2.
Advisor: Great. And employment status-wise—Rober, you’re currently unemployed, and Elizabeth you’re currently employed. Still accurate?
Client 1: Yes.
Client 2: Yes.

Client 2: Yes, okay, $48,650. I always round it up in my head.
Advisor: Perfect—$48,650 gross for you, Elizabeth. Thanks for the correction, Rober.
Advisor: And Rober, your background—what kind of work is it? Like industry or function—finance, tech, healthcare?
Client 1: Finance. Corporate finance—FP&A type stuff.
Client 2: He says finance, but it’s not like Wall Street trading or anything.

Advisor: Fair. Okay—back to it.
